body.x: Quantum clustering is not about doing ordinary arithmetic faster.

Instead it borrows the real physical behaviour of nature — particles that hold several states at once, waves that add up or cancel, and particles that stay linked as one system — to judge how alike two pieces of data are.
Those judgements live in a geometry that ordinary step-by-step computers cannot lay out efficiently, so the physics itself does the work the silicon cannot.

defs.def: A quantum system's ability to hold several possibilities at the same time, instead of being forced to pick just one.
The way quantum possibilities add together or cancel out, like ripples on a pond reinforcing or flattening each other.
A link between quantum particles so tight that they behave as one connected system no matter how far apart they are handled.
The standard design of ordinary computers, which fetch and process one instruction after another in sequence.

body.x: An ordinary computer bit is a simple switch: it is either a 0 or a 1, with nothing in between.
A qubit — the quantum version of a bit — is allowed to sit in a blend of 0 and 1 at the same time, a condition physicists call superposition.
What the blend actually means
The blend is described by two quantities, one tied to the 0 and one to the 1, and squaring each of them gives the chance of seeing that outcome when the qubit is finally measured.
Those two chances always add up to exactly one, because the qubit must land on some answer whenever it is read.
The blend is genuine, not just a gap in knowledge: right up until the moment of measurement the qubit really is holding both possibilities together.
b: genuine, not just a gap in knowledge

body.x: The Bloch-sphere picture
A handy way to picture a single qubit is as an arrow pointing somewhere on the surface of a globe.

The north pole stands for a pure 0 and the south pole for a pure 1, while every other direction on the globe is a specific mixture of the two, so a qubit carries a continuous direction rather than a single yes-or-no.
The instant a qubit is measured, that rich direction collapses down to just one plain bit, either 0 or 1.

defs.def: The quantum version of a bit: instead of being just 0 or 1, it can hold a blend of both at once until it is measured.
The condition of a qubit holding 0 and 1 together at the same time, before any measurement forces a single answer.
One of the two quantities describing a qubit's blend; squaring it gives the chance of seeing that particular outcome.
A globe used to picture a single qubit, where every direction on its surface stands for a different blend of 0 and 1.

body.x: The real power of quantum computing shows up when many qubits are joined together.
Adding qubits does not merely add capacity, it multiplies it: each extra qubit doubles the number of separate quantities the machine is juggling at once.
Just fifty qubits already hold roughly a thousand trillion of these quantities, all evolving together as one linked system — a scale no ordinary memory could ever store.
The resource
This enormous space, doubling with every qubit added, is the raw resource that makes quantum machines attractive for problems with huge numbers of dimensions.
The honest catch
Reading the machine destroys almost all of that richness: a single measurement returns only as many plain bits as there are qubits, so the vast hidden interior can never be read out directly.
b: only as many plain bits as there are qubits

body.x: Because of this, a quantum algorithm cannot simply dump out its answer; it must be built so that the one useful result is the outcome most likely to appear when the qubits are finally measured.
The tool for that is interference — arranging the waves inside the machine so that wrong answers cancel each other out and the right answer reinforces itself.
Being upfront about this limit is exactly what separates honest quantum computing from hype.

defs.def: One of the many internal quantities a quantum machine tracks; there are twice as many of them for every extra qubit.
The act of reading a quantum machine, which forces its rich hidden state down to a plain string of ordinary bits.
The deliberate arranging of quantum waves so that wrong answers cancel and the desired answer stands out clearly.

body.x: Before any quantum method can help, the plain numbers in a dataset have to be loaded into a quantum machine, and there are two main ways to do it.
Other loading schemes exist, but these two carry the essential story.

defs.def: A way of loading data that packs many numbers into a quantum state's internal quantities, using very few qubits.
A way of loading data that turns each feature into the rotation of a qubit, like setting a dial by the data's value.
The quantum version of a bit, able to hold a blend of 0 and 1 rather than just one or the other.
Today's early quantum computers, which are noisy and can only run short circuits before errors pile up.

cards: Amplitude encoding pours the numbers of a data point directly into the internal quantities of a quantum state, packing many values into very few qubits.
A data point with a thousand features needs only about ten qubits, because each added qubit doubles how much can be stored — an exponential compression.
This makes it the natural choice whenever the goal is to squeeze very high-dimensional data down to a handful of qubits.
The price is that preparing such a tightly packed state is delicate and hard to do reliably on today's noisy machines.
Angle encoding takes each feature and uses it to set how far a qubit is rotated, much like turning a dial by an amount the data decides.
It uses more qubits per data point than amplitude encoding, but the circuits it needs are short and simple.
That shallowness makes it well suited to today's near-term hardware, which loses accuracy as circuits grow longer.
It is the route taken by the live notebook demonstration in this session.

body.x: A quantum feature map is the step that gives quantum clustering its real edge.
It takes each data point and, through a small tunable circuit, spreads it out into an enormously large mathematical space — far larger than the original short list of features.
In that expanded space, groups that were hopelessly tangled together in the raw data can fall into cleanly separate regions, the way a crumpled sheet becomes easy to divide once it is unfolded.
Why the shape of the circuit matters
The circuits that work best deliberately tie the qubits together — an effect called entanglement — and richer, well-entangled maps have been shown to produce steadier, more reliable clusters.
The central bet
The whole advantage rests on a single wager: that the map reaches into a space so complex that an ordinary computer could not fake the same similarities without an impractical amount of work.
When that bet holds, the quantum machine is measuring likenesses that no classical shortcut can cheaply reproduce.
b: no classical shortcut can cheaply reproduce

body.x: If an ordinary computer can imitate the map cheaply, the quantum advantage simply disappears — so the choice of map is everything.

defs.def: A small tunable circuit that spreads each data point into a vastly larger space where hidden groupings become easier to separate.
The mathematical space a quantum system lives in, which grows astronomically large as more qubits are added.
A deep linking of qubits so their behaviours are tied together, which gives a feature map much of its richness.
Two well-known recipes for wiring up a feature map's circuit, favoured because they tend to give steadier clusters.

body.x: These two numbers are early evidence that a quantum notion of similarity can hold its own on real, well-known datasets.
What the numbers say

On the Iris dataset — a classic table of flower measurements long used to test clustering methods — swapping the ordinary distance for a quantum-kernel similarity sorted the flowers correctly about 88.6% of the time.
On a breast-cancer dataset, the same quantum-similarity approach reached about 91.0% agreement with the true groupings.
Why it matters
The striking part is that these scores came from short, simple circuits — the kind today's imperfect hardware can actually run — not from some far-future ideal machine.
That suggests a quantum feature map offers a genuinely richer way of judging similarity than a plain straight-line distance.
These figures are competitive with classical methods, not a landslide — the honest reading is promising, not a knockout.

defs.def: A measure of how similar two data points are, computed by comparing their quantum states rather than their straight-line distance.
A particular recipe for encoding data into a quantum state, named after the mathematical symmetry it is built on.
A small, famous table of flower measurements widely used as a testing ground for clustering methods.
The fraction of data points a method places into the correct natural group.

body.x: At its heart, quantum clustering rests on one simple fact about what a quantum computer naturally produces.
When two data points are loaded into quantum states, the machine readily hands back a single number that measures how much the two states overlap — how closely aligned they are.
Overlap is similarity
That overlap number is exactly a measure of similarity: it runs high when two points are alike and drops toward zero when they point in completely different directions.
b: runs high when two points are alike

body.x: This is what makes it so useful, because every clustering method, classical or quantum alike, is ultimately built on some notion of how similar two things are.
From similarity to distance
Ordinary distance is only one short step away: once the overlap is known, a simple relationship turns that similarity straight into the familiar straight-line distance between the two points.
So the entire task shrinks down to one thing — building a small quantum circuit that turns this overlap into a number that can actually be measured.
That small circuit is the swap test, the workhorse examined on the slides that follow.
This overlap-equals-similarity idea is the single hinge on which the rest of the quantum clustering story turns.

defs.def: A single number measuring how aligned two quantum states are — large when they are alike, near zero when they are unrelated.
Any measure of how alike two data points are, which every clustering method needs in some form.
The full description of a quantum system, into which a data point is loaded so the machine can work with it.
A small quantum circuit that turns the overlap between two states into a probability that can be measured.

body.x: 2025 is the year quantum computing stopped being a promise on a whiteboard and became a measured, peer-reviewed fact.
What actually changed

For years, the claim that a quantum machine could beat an ordinary computer was mostly theory. In a single year, three separate results — each checked by independent referees and published in the world's top journals — showed it happening for real.

Google's Willow chip (105 qubits, published in Nature in December 2024) was the first to cross a crucial line: as engineers made its error-correcting code larger, the error rate dropped by roughly a factor of two at each step instead of climbing.

Google's "Quantum Echoes" result (Nature, October 2025) ran a calculation about 13,000 times faster than the best classical approach, and — a first — the answer can be independently re-checked on a second machine.

D-Wave's Advantage2 machine (Science, March 2025) simulated the behaviour of magnetic materials in minutes, a genuinely useful scientific problem, though later classical rebuttals leave this claim contested.
Why a faculty room should care
These are not consumer gadgets; they are early proof that a genuinely new kind of computing tool works. That reframes quantum from a physics curiosity into something teachable and testable.
The timeline turns quantum computing into a subject students across engineering, finance, biology and data science will be asked about — and faculty are the first people they will ask.
b: faculty are the first people they will ask

body.x: Honest framing: these are milestones on narrow benchmark problems, not everyday general-purpose speedups yet.

defs.def: The quantum version of a bit: instead of being only 0 or 1, it can hold a blend of both at once until it is measured.
A demonstration that a quantum machine solves some specific problem faster or better than the best available ordinary computer.
A way of bundling many imperfect qubits into one protected qubit so that random errors cancel out instead of piling up; "below threshold" means bigger bundles actually make errors smaller.
Checked and approved by independent expert referees before publication, which is why these results count as verified fact rather than a company press release.

body.x: Three results, three different machines, one year — here is what each one actually did, and why it counts.

defs.def: The quantum version of a bit that can hold a blend of 0 and 1 at once; the raw building block of a quantum machine.
A measure of how large and protective an error-correcting code is; "distance-7" is a bigger, sturdier bundle of qubits than distance-3.
A specialised quantum machine that solves a problem by physically settling into its lowest-energy state, like a ball rolling to the bottom of a valley, rather than running general programs.
A physics technique that sends a signal forward and then backward through a quantum system to measure how quickly information gets scrambled — the trick behind the Quantum Echoes result.

cards: Willow is a 105-qubit superconducting chip from Google, unveiled in Nature in December 2024.

Its headline is not raw speed but reliability: it was the first machine to run error correction below threshold, meaning the more physical qubits were bundled into one protected qubit, the lower the error became.

At the largest code size tested, called distance-7, each step up in size suppressed the error by a factor of about 2.14 — the first time in history errors shrank instead of growing as the machine got bigger.
This matters because scaling up without drowning in errors is the single biggest obstacle between today's machines and genuinely useful ones.
Quantum Echoes, from Google and published in Nature in October 2025, is the first quantum advantage that is verifiable — the result can be independently confirmed rather than merely asserted.
It ran on 65 of Willow's qubits and used a method called out-of-time-order correlators, a way of pushing a signal forward and back through the system to measure how information scrambles.
The quantum machine reached its answer about 13,000 times faster than the best classical method for the same task.
Because the output can be cross-checked on a second machine, it points toward practical uses such as reading out the structure of molecules.
Advantage2 is a D-Wave quantum annealer with more than 4,400 qubits, reported in Science in March 2025.
An annealer is not a general-purpose computer; it is a specialised machine that relaxes into the lowest-energy answer to a problem, like a ball rolling to the bottom of a valley.
It simulated the dynamics of spin-glass magnetic materials in minutes — a real scientific target rather than a toy benchmark.
Honesty demands a caveat: parts of the result were later reproduced on ordinary GPUs, so this particular claim remains contested.

body.x: The conversation has shifted from betting on whether quantum works to positioning for when it pays off — and the money shows it.
What the tiles are saying
These figures come from major consultancies — McKinsey's 2026 Quantum Technology Monitor and BCG — and are best read as orders of magnitude, the scale and direction of the trend, not exact forecasts.
McKinsey estimates quantum computing could unlock between 1.3 and 2.7 trillion dollars of economic value worldwide by 2035, concentrated in chemicals, life sciences, finance and mobility.
b: 1.3 and 2.7 trillion dollars of economic value

body.x: Investors poured 12.6 billion dollars into quantum start-ups in 2025 — 6.3 times the previous year — a jump that marks the field crossing from research into commerce.
Quantum-computing companies earned more than 1 billion dollars in real revenue in 2025, projected to reach about 4.4 billion by 2028 — money changing hands for actual services, not grants.
b: more than 1 billion dollars in real revenue

body.x: BCG's independent estimate points to 450 to 850 billion dollars of economic value by 2040 — a different method reaching the same conclusion, that the curve bends sharply upward.
Where the value sits — and why it matters here
The value is not mainly in selling the physical processors; it is in the software, the algorithms and the AI that turn a quantum machine into a usable answer.
That value-capturing layer is a keyboard-and-brains problem rather than a chip-fabrication problem, which is precisely where India's deep pool of software and mathematical talent has its edge.
b: India's deep pool of software and mathematical talent

body.x: These are projections, not certainties; the honest use is the scale and direction they point to, not the exact number.

defs.def: The total extra worth quantum is expected to add across whole industries — savings, new products, better decisions — not the money the quantum companies themselves earn.
The actual money a quantum company takes in from selling its products and services; a much smaller, more concrete number than economy-wide value.
A rough size given to the nearest factor of ten; it says whether something is in the billions or the trillions, without claiming the exact figure.

body.x: The whole reason a faculty-development programme on quantum exists comes down to one shortage: people who understand this technology.
The numbers, in plain terms
Industry projects roughly 250,000 new quantum jobs by 2030 and around 840,000 by 2035 — a workforce that does not yet exist.
The supply is tiny: in 2025 there were about three job openings for every qualified candidate, and roughly half of that year's quantum roles simply went unfilled.
b: three job openings for every qualified candidate
half of that year's quantum roles simply went unfilled

body.x: A shortage this steep is not a mere hiring inconvenience; the United States has publicly called it a national-security vulnerability.
Why this lands in a faculty room
Jobs are created by industry, but qualified people are created in classrooms — which places faculty at the exact chokepoint of the entire field.
Every course that introduces quantum machine learning, and every student pointed toward it, directly widens the supply that industry is desperate for.
For teachers, that gap is a clear mandate; for their students, it is an unusually open door into a high-demand field.
These are industry projections; the exact counts will move, but the pattern — demand far outrunning supply — is consistent across every source.

defs.def: Using quantum computers to help find patterns in data, the subject this programme prepares faculty to teach.
A short intensive course that trains university teachers themselves, so they can go back and teach a new subject to their students.
The distance between how many trained people a field needs and how many actually exist; here, hundreds of thousands of quantum jobs with almost no one qualified to fill them.

body.x: Most of the world's data arrives with no answer key attached — and that single fact is why unsupervised learning matters.
The label problem
A label is a human-supplied tag that says what a piece of data is — 'this scan shows a tumour', 'this transaction is fraud'. The trouble is that most data never gets one.
Roughly 80 percent of real-world data is unlabelled, because labelling is expensive, needs scarce experts, or is simply impossible at the scale data now arrives.
b: 80 percent of real-world data is unlabelled

body.x: When there is no answer key, a method cannot be told what to look for — it has to discover the structure on its own. That is exactly what clustering does: it makes the first sense of a brand-new pile of data.
The scale is staggering
The Square Kilometre Array radio telescope will pour out data at the rate of petabytes every second — a raw flow larger than the entire current internet.
The upgraded Large Hadron Collider will archive exabytes of collision data every year, far more than any team could ever label by hand.
b: exabytes of collision data every year

body.x: A single RNA-sequencing run measures more than 20,000 genes at once, with no preset grouping of which genes belong together.
In every one of these cases the task is geometric: find which points sit near each other in a vast, high-dimensional space, and those neighbourhoods reveal the hidden groups.
Unlabelled does not mean meaningless — the structure is genuinely there; it simply has to be uncovered rather than looked up.

defs.def: A human-added tag stating what a data point is (cat, tumour, fraud); supervised methods need them, but most real data has none.
Sorting data into natural groups of similar items without being told in advance what the groups are.
Enormous units of data — a petabyte is about a million gigabytes, and an exabyte is a thousand times larger still.
A lab technique that reads the activity of tens of thousands of genes in a sample at once, producing a huge unlabelled table of numbers.

body.x: Unsupervised learning is, in one sentence, finding structure in data that has no labels.
The coins-in-the-dark picture
Picture tipping a bag of mixed coins onto a table in a dark room. The denominations cannot be read, but the size and weight of each coin can still be felt by hand.
Sorting those coins into piles by feel alone — grouping the ones that seem alike, with no idea what any pile is 'worth' — is exactly what clustering does.
The machine is never told the right answer. It is handed a pile of points and asked only which ones belong together, using nothing but a measure of how similar any two points are.
Where it sits on the machine-learning map
Supervised learning is the opposite situation: the data comes with labels, and the goal is to copy the mapping — for example, learning from 10,000 labelled tumours how to classify the next one.
Unsupervised learning gets the data with no labels, and the goal is to discover structure — asking, of those same 10,000 tumours, whether there are natural subtypes nobody named in advance.
Reinforcement learning is a third mode entirely: an agent learns by trial and error, taking actions and adjusting from rewards and penalties, the way a player gradually improves at a game.
This talk lives squarely in the middle branch — grouping unlabelled data — which is where the coins-in-the-dark problem, and quantum clustering, both belong.
The three modes are not rivals; most real systems mix them, but the unlabelled case is the one where quantum offers the clearest new leverage.

defs.def: Finding patterns and groups in data that carries no answer key, discovering structure rather than being taught it.
Learning from labelled examples, so the machine can copy the pattern and label new data it has never seen.
Learning by trial and error, where an agent takes actions and improves from rewards and penalties, like getting better at a game.
The unsupervised task of sorting data into natural groups of similar items, using only a measure of how alike two points are.

body.x: Strip away the names, and every clustering method — classical or quantum — comes down to just three choices.
The spine of the whole session

Holding these three ingredients in mind makes the quantum part simple to place: quantum methods leave the first and third choices mostly untouched and change the second one — how similarity is measured, and how fast it can be computed.

defs.def: Turning each object into a list of numbers so it becomes a point in space that a computer can compare.
The ordinary straight-line distance between two points, the most common way of measuring how far apart they are.
A flexible similarity rule that can judge points as alike even when a simple straight-line distance would call them far apart.
The average point of a cluster — its centre of gravity — that many methods use to represent the whole group.

cards: Representation means turning each object into a list of numbers, so it becomes a point in space that a computer can handle.
A customer might become a point made of age, spending and visit frequency; an image becomes the long list of its pixel brightnesses.
The choice of which features to include quietly decides what 'close together' will even mean later on.
Good representation is half the battle: the right features make natural groups obvious, while the wrong ones hide them completely.
Similarity is the rule for deciding how alike two points are — the numerical answer to the question 'how close?'.
Everyday choices include straight-line Euclidean distance, the angle between two points known as cosine similarity, or a more flexible kernel.
This single number drives everything: change the notion of similarity and the very same data can fall into completely different groups.
This is the ingredient quantum computing mostly changes, offering a new, physically-computed way to measure how alike two points are.
The objective is the definition of a good grouping — the target the method actually tries to hit.
A common objective is to make the points inside each cluster as tightly packed as possible while pushing different clusters far apart.
It is what the algorithm optimises; k-means, for instance, tries to minimise how far points sit from the centre of their own cluster.
Different objectives suit different data shapes, which is exactly why no single clustering method wins everywhere.

body.x: India runs a single national programme dedicated entirely to quantum technology, funded and scheduled by the central government.
What it is
The mission was approved on 19 April 2023 with a budget of ₹6,003.65 crore, roughly 0.73 billion US dollars, and runs for eight years, from 2023-24 to 2030-31.
This is a fixed, time-bound national bet with clear deadlines, not an open-ended promise to fund quantum forever.
The staged hardware targets
The plan does not try to build one giant machine overnight; it grows the hardware in deliberate steps so each stage can be tested before the next.
A working machine of 20 to 50 quantum bits is targeted by year three, 50 to 100 by year five, and anywhere from 50 up to 1,000 by year eight.

Two different physical recipes are pursued in parallel, superconducting circuits chilled to near absolute zero and photonic devices that compute with particles of light, so the country is not betting on a single technology.
A national footprint

The effort spans 152 researchers across 43 institutions in 17 states and two union territories, organised into 14 technical groups, reach that touches essentially every region represented in the room.
These qubit counts are physical qubits, the raw, error-prone building blocks, not the far scarcer error-corrected qubits needed for a fully reliable machine.

defs.def: The quantum version of a bit: instead of being only 0 or 1, it can hold a blend of both at once until it is measured.
A tiny electrical loop cooled to near absolute zero so current flows with zero resistance, one of the leading ways to build a qubit.
Hardware that carries and processes quantum information using individual particles of light instead of electrical circuits.
A single real, imperfect qubit; many of them must be pooled together to make one dependable error-corrected qubit.

body.x: The mission is not run from one lab; the work is divided among four national hubs, each led by a top institute and each responsible for one theme.
The design is called hub-and-spoke: a lead institute anchors each theme and coordinates partner institutes around it, so effort is shared instead of duplicated.
All four hubs were set up as Section-8 not-for-profit companies and have been operational since October 2024.

defs.def: An organisation where one central lead (the hub) coordinates many connected partners (the spokes), like an airline routing flights through a main airport.
An Indian legal form for a not-for-profit organisation, meaning any surplus is reinvested in the mission rather than paid out as profit.
A way to share secret codes so that the laws of physics reveal any eavesdropper the instant someone tries to listen in.
A clock that keeps time by counting the ultra-steady vibrations of atoms, so accurate it would not lose a second over billions of years.

cards: This hub, anchored at IISc Bengaluru, is charged with building India's own quantum processors rather than importing them.
It owns the staged hardware goals, growing from a few dozen qubits toward the eventual 1,000-qubit target over the eight years.
Both the superconducting and the photonic machine designs fall under its responsibility.
Led jointly by IIT Madras and C-DOT in New Delhi, this hub builds communication links that are secure by the laws of physics.
Its focus is quantum key distribution, which lets two parties detect any eavesdropper on the line automatically.
The roadmap stretches from city-to-city links on existing fibre to satellite links spanning about 2,000 kilometres.
Based at IIT Bombay, this hub develops instruments that measure the physical world with extreme precision.
Its products include magnetometers, atomic clocks, and gravity sensors far more sensitive than conventional devices.
Such sensors matter for navigation, mineral and resource surveys, and medical imaging.
Anchored at IIT Delhi, this hub creates the raw materials and cold-running components that qubits are actually made from.
It works on superconductors, exotic topological materials, and semiconductor structures for device fabrication.
Without this materials layer the other three hubs would have nothing to build with.

body.x: Beyond the government mission, Indian companies and labs have already shipped working quantum hardware and networks, not just plans and slideware.

defs.def: A machine where a single company builds the whole tower, from the physical qubits at the bottom to the control software at the top.
A way to build a quantum computer by holding individual charged atoms still with electric fields and manipulating them with laser light.
New codes designed to stay secure even against a future quantum computer, so today's data cannot be unlocked once such machines arrive.

cards: QpiAI, a Bengaluru full-stack startup, shipped Indus, a 25-qubit machine, on World Quantum Day in April 2025.
It followed with Kaveri, a 64-qubit machine, in November 2025, with commercial access planned for late 2026.
The company has raised about 65.6 million US dollars and employs around 100 people.
Its stated roadmap reaches 1,000 qubits by 2030.
Launched on 7 February 2026 as a partnership of IBM, TCS, and the Andhra Pradesh government.
Its centrepiece is an IBM Quantum System Two built around a 156-qubit Heron processor, the largest quantum computer sited in India so far.
Commissioning is expected around September 2026, with L&T handling the physical infrastructure and more than 50 partners involved.
IISER Pune is building a 20-qubit ion-trap machine due by the end of 2026, having already trapped 25 calcium ions.
In April 2026 an indigenous system from QNu Labs demonstrated secure quantum communication over roughly 1,000 kilometres.
The mission's funded startup layer grew from 8 to 17 companies, including QNu Labs, Dimira, Quanastra, and Prenishq.
A national post-quantum-cryptography roadmap was published in February 2026 to protect Aadhaar-scale identity and UPI-scale payments before the threat matures.

body.x: India's quantum budget is small next to the biggest spenders, and the honest way to read that is as a deliberate strategy, not a failure.
The numbers, side by side

India's roughly 0.73 billion US dollars over eight years sits against China's reputed 15 billion, the United States' 2.5 billion-plus, the United Kingdom's 2.5 billion pounds over ten years, and Germany's two-to-three billion euros.
On money alone India is not in the top tier, and pretending otherwise would convince no one who works in the field.
Why that is not the whole story
A budget this size cannot fund every frontier at once, so it is aimed at a few chosen niches instead of trying to outbuild anyone.
India's real advantage is not spending but a very large pool of skilled software and algorithm talent, which is the layer where most of the eventual economic value is captured.

Building a quantum processor is expensive hardware work, but inventing the algorithms and applications that run on any processor is mostly a matter of trained minds and keyboards, which plays directly to that strength.
The topics of this session, clustering, quantum kernels, and quantum-enhanced vision, sit squarely in that value-capturing algorithm-and-application layer.
The cross-country figures are approximate estimates gathered from public reporting and are best read as orders of magnitude, not an exact league table.

defs.def: Sorting a pile of data into natural groups based on similarity, without anyone labelling the groups in advance.
A way of measuring how similar two data points are by running them through a quantum circuit, giving a richer notion of similarity than ordinary distance.
The software and methods that run on top of the hardware, where clever ideas, rather than expensive machines, create most of the value.

body.x: Viksit Bharat 2047 is the national goal of a fully developed India by 2047, the hundredth year of independence, and quantum technology is named as one of its pillars.
The proof India can do this
India has already taken frontier technology to more than a billion people through Aadhaar for identity, UPI for real-time payments, and the wider Digital India programme.
That track record shows the country can run brand-new technology at population scale, which is exactly what a national quantum ecosystem will demand.
The leap being asked for
The next step is to move from being the world's back office to the world's innovation engine, which needs deep original research, not only frugal efficiency.

Quantum sits inside a coordinated set of national missions, the National Quantum Mission alongside IndiaAI, the National Supercomputing Mission, Semicon India, and a research fund of one lakh crore rupees.
Here sovereignty means owning secure communications and home-built hardware, rather than renting quantum computing from foreign cloud providers.
The stated ambition treats technology as a strategic national asset for growth, security, governance, and global influence.

defs.def: India's stated goal of becoming a fully developed nation by 2047, the hundredth anniversary of its independence.
Nationwide digital systems, like Aadhaar and UPI, that everyone can use as shared public utilities, the way roads or electricity are shared.
A country's ability to own and control its critical technology at home, instead of depending on foreign providers for it.
An Indian unit for a very large number: one lakh crore equals one trillion, so a one-lakh-crore fund is a trillion rupees.

cards: To reach the 2047 goal, India's research-and-development spending must climb toward two to three percent of GDP, well above today's level.
The country needs to reverse its brain drain and create enough high-tech jobs to keep skilled people at home.
Every faculty member is a direct lever on the talent side of that equation.
The cohort now sitting in the room's classrooms is who will actually build Viksit Bharat 2047.

body.x: A first-principles tour of clustering for a faculty audience drawn from many different fields: it begins with tools already taught in class, adds one quantum idea at a time, and ends with two demonstrations that run on any ordinary laptop.

defs.def: A way of computing that uses the strange rules governing nature's smallest particles to perform certain calculations that ordinary computers find impractical.
A branch of machine learning that hunts for patterns in data that comes with no labels and no answer key.
Sorting a pile of data into groups whose members resemble one another, without being told in advance what the groups should be.

body.x: This session builds from an everyday idea to a working quantum demonstration in five moves, and each move starts from something already familiar rather than something exotic.
The five-part arc
The first section asks why quantum machine learning deserves attention right now, leaning on peer-reviewed results and market figures from 2025 rather than on hype.
The second section revisits the classical clustering tools already on every machine-learning syllabus, k-means and spectral clustering, and shows precisely where they break down.
The third section introduces just enough quantum mechanics to explain one single new trick: judging how alike two data points are through physics instead of ordinary arithmetic.
That same section stays deliberately honest, running two live notebooks and naming every place the quantum advantage is genuine and every place it is merely competitive.
The fourth section surveys where quantum clustering already runs today, from commercial pilots in finance and pharma to the largest physics experiments ever built.
The fifth and sixth sections turn to India's national quantum programme and to the part a room full of teachers plays in closing the worldwide talent shortage.
Nothing along this path requires access to a quantum computer in order to follow it, because every quantum idea is anchored to a classical one first.

defs.def: A branch of machine learning that finds structure in data that carries no labels telling it the right answer.
Grouping data so that things inside a group are alike and things in different groups are not, without being given the groups beforehand.
A classic, fast method that splits data into a fixed number of groups by repeatedly nudging each group's centre toward the points nearest to it.

body.x: Archit Srivastava sits at the meeting point of industry data work and quantum computing, and has spent roughly four years turning quantum research into things that actually run rather than things that merely get discussed.
His current role is Senior Manager of Data Engineering at PUMA in Bengaluru, where the everyday work is building the large-scale data systems a global company depends on.
Industry background

Before PUMA he worked as a data-engineering consultant at o9 Solutions and as a data engineer at Hewlett Packard Enterprise, and at both companies he ran quantum proof-of-concept projects alongside the mainstream work.
His first foothold in the field came as one of the earliest interns at BosonQ Psi, a start-up applying quantum methods to engineering and physics simulation.
Building India's quantum community
In 2020 he founded AiQyaM, described as India's first quantum-hardware community, and he went on to found CIRQuIT Quantum Research at RV College of Engineering.

He has served as Senior Research Associate and head of the quantum-hardware vertical at Quantum Computing India, and has contributed proof-of-concept frameworks to the GKQCTP and IQDC quantum-technology-park efforts with Innogress Ventures.
His formal training is a degree in Electronics and Instrumentation Engineering from RV College of Engineering, an unusual route into quantum computing that keeps the emphasis firmly on real devices.
The consistent thread through the whole record is hands-on hardware and community-building, not theory in isolation.

What he works on

His current research sits where quantum computing meets artificial intelligence, covering black-hole physics and gravitational waves studied with quantum and AI methods, together with photonic quantum computing and its hardware.

He has authored five or more papers and book chapters, indexed on Google Scholar, including one with a direct gravitational-wave tie-in titled Quantum Computing and LIGO.

His 2021 paper Quantum Finance — An Overview mapped an investment-portfolio problem onto a quantum optimiser and, tellingly for this audience, solved the very same problem in both Qiskit and PennyLane, the two teaching frameworks this room must choose between.
He has given twenty-five or more invited talks across ten or more universities, engaging more than two hundred faculty, and spoke at Conf42 Quantum Computing in both 2023 and 2024.
Because the demonstrations shown later are drawn from circuits he has run on real quantum hardware, the limitations named in this session are reported first-hand, not repeated from someone else.

body.x: The session follows one simple teaching rule: never introduce a quantum idea cold, and always attach it to a classical idea already taught in the classroom.
The design principle
Each new quantum concept appears only after its everyday classical counterpart is already on the board, so nothing in the talk asks for a leap of faith.

The path runs left to right through five stops: the idea of unsupervised learning, the classical tools, the single quantum turn, an honest look at what it does and does not deliver, and finally the real-world impact.
The spine of the whole talk

Every clustering method, classical or quantum alike, comes down to exactly three choices: how each data point is written as numbers, how the similarity between two points is measured, and what rule decides the final groups.

The one sentence that carries the entire session is that quantum computing mostly changes the middle choice, the way the similarity between two points is measured, and how quickly that measurement can be made.

Because only that single ingredient changes, the rest of the familiar pipeline stays in place, which is exactly why a classical clustering method can accept a quantum-computed similarity without being rebuilt.
Both demonstrations run as live PennyLane notebooks on an ordinary laptop processor, so no quantum hardware is needed to follow along.

defs.def: The way a piece of data is written down as numbers so that a computer can work with it.
A single number saying how alike two data points are; the whole of clustering rests on measuring it well.
A small quantum procedure that measures how alike two quantum-encoded data points are, returning a number near one when they match and near one-half when they are unrelated.
A similarity score between two data points that is worked out by a quantum circuit instead of by ordinary arithmetic.

body.x: For years quantum computing lived mostly in the laboratory as a promise. In 2025 and 2026 it stepped into paid, real-world pilot projects, and these four numbers mark the shift from asking whether it will matter to planning for when.
The change in tone is the real story. Businesses stopped debating if quantum machines would ever be useful and started modelling exactly when the technology will reshape their own industry.
More than 300 large companies are now actively running quantum projects, according to McKinsey's 2026 tracking, a clear sign this has moved well past a handful of research labs.
Investment into quantum start-ups jumped 6.3 times from 2024 to 2025, one of the sharpest funding surges any young technology has seen.
Roughly 90% of that money went specifically into quantum computing, rather than the neighbouring fields of quantum sensing or quantum communication.
Revenue from quantum-computing companies, already above one billion dollars in 2025, is projected to reach about 4.4 billion dollars by 2028.
The common thread across every sector
The industries adopting quantum first all share one shape of problem: enormous piles of data with no labels attached, where the costly step is measuring how similar one item is to another.
Finance, pharmaceuticals and climate technology are the commercial vanguard, while the giant physics experiments run the hardest versions of the very same clustering task.
Most of these remain early pilots and competitive results, not yet routine wins over the very best classical methods.

defs.def: A way of computing that uses the strange rules of the very small, where a particle can be in several states at once, to attack problems ordinary computers find impossibly large.
Sorting a pile of data into natural groups by similarity, without anyone labelling the groups in advance.
Raw information that has not been tagged with the right answer, so a computer must uncover the structure on its own.
A single number saying how alike two data points are; computing it for every pair is the expensive step quantum methods aim to speed up.

body.x: Banks live and die by fast, accurate answers drawn from huge streams of numbers, so finance became the first industry to move quantum methods into real pilots.
Across pricing, fraud and hedging the underlying job is the same: find hidden structure and measure similarity inside data far too large and tangled for easy classical handling.
These are demonstrations and early pilots; the quantum step usually assists a classical system rather than replacing it outright.

defs.def: Estimating an answer by running a process with random inputs many thousands of times and averaging the results.
A single grade from 0 to 1 that balances catching the real cases against avoiding false alarms; higher is better.
A grid showing, for every pair of things, whether they tend to move up and down together or in opposite directions.
A widely used free software toolkit from IBM for writing and running programs on quantum computers.

cards: Banks price complex products such as options by simulating thousands of possible futures at random, a method called Monte Carlo, and more precision means far more simulations.
With the classical method, getting ten times more accurate costs roughly a hundred times more computing, so the error shrinks painfully slowly.
JPMorgan Chase and IBM Quantum used a quantum technique that reaches the same precision with far fewer runs, close to the square root of the classical count.
The payoff is concrete: a bank can price the same risk much faster than rivals, or spend the same time and walk away with a far more confident number.
Fraudsters constantly change tactics, creating twisted, non-obvious patterns that classical detectors struggle to separate from honest spending.
IBM's Safer Payments team, using the Qiskit toolkit, encoded each transaction into a quantum state so that honest and fraudulent behaviour land in clearly separate regions.
One quantum classifier reached an F1 score as high as 0.88, a balanced measure of catching fraud without wrongly flagging good customers.
The models stayed accurate even when five different kinds of hardware noise were added on purpose, showing they are robust enough for near-term use.
In practice the quantum step selects the most revealing features and hands them to a fast classical system for the final decision.
Hedging means holding offsetting positions so a portfolio can survive sudden market swings, and doing it well is a hard balancing problem.
JPMorgan Chase and the software firm QC Ware studied quantum deep hedging, which adjusts that protection dynamically as markets move.
Deciding how to spread money across many assets is itself an unsupervised task: uncovering hidden structure in how the assets move together.
That structure is captured in a covariance matrix, a large grid of how every pair of assets rises and falls in step, exactly the similarity problem quantum methods target.

body.x: Living things are quantum at their core, and biology produces gigantic piles of data with no answer key, which makes medicine a natural home for quantum clustering.
Reading the genome without labels
Genomics may be the hardest clustering problem of all, since it means deciding which of tens of thousands of genes truly matter with nobody supplying the correct groupings.

Dynamic Quantum Clustering sorted raw gene-activity readings across more than 20,057 genes into groups that matched the known progression from low-grade to aggressive brain tumours, doing so without being shown a single diagnosis, and agreeing with real clinical outcomes about 90.9% of the time.
Narrowing those 20,057 genes down to the 90 that drove the separation lifted diagnostic accuracy to 97.3%, still using no labels at all.
Simulating the molecules themselves
Because chemistry is itself governed by quantum rules, a quantum computer can model a drug molecule more faithfully than the rough approximations classical machines are forced to use.

Algorithmiq and Quantum Circuits ran enzyme-behaviour simulations on a small 8-qubit machine built from dual-rail qubits, which flag their own mistakes, since a lost particle of light shows up as a marked result that can simply be discarded.
This correct-first-then-scale approach aims at de novo drug design: building a promising molecule from scratch rather than only screening ones that already exist.
These are early, specialised results on small hardware, not yet everyday drug-discovery pipelines.

defs.def: The quantum version of a bit: instead of being only 0 or 1, it can hold a blend of both at once until it is measured.
A lab method that reads how active each gene is in a sample, producing tens of thousands of numbers per patient.
A quantum method that groups data by its natural geometry, needing no labels and no advance guess at how many groups to expect.
Designing a brand-new medicine molecule from scratch to fit a target, rather than testing molecules that already exist.

body.x: Cutting carbon and running cleaner power grids both come down to understanding molecules and messy sensor data, two places where quantum methods are starting to help.
Chemistry is quantum at its core and grid data is vast and unlabelled, so both the molecular modelling and the data sorting play to quantum strengths.
Most of these are promising early results and simulations, not yet drop-in replacements for classical tools.

defs.def: A sponge-like material with a repeating, adjustable cage structure whose tiny pores can trap specific gas molecules.
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction while itself staying unchanged and reusable.
A classic method that sorts data into a set number of groups by repeatedly assigning each point to its nearest group centre.

cards: One way to fight climate change is to trap carbon dioxide before it escapes a smokestack, which needs cheap materials that can grab the gas.
Metal-organic frameworks, nicknamed molecular LEGO, are sponge-like structures with tunable pores that soak up carbon dioxide using very little energy.
Classical computers struggle to model exactly how the gas sticks inside those pores, because many particles interact all at once.
Quantinuum, working with TotalEnergies, broke the giant calculation into smaller quantum fragments to model the binding accurately.
A companion tool called PrISMa blends machine learning with quantum-informed simulation to predict a material's cost and performance almost instantly.
A catalyst speeds up a chemical reaction without being used up, and modern industry leans on them almost everywhere.
The catalysts that make ammonia for fertiliser account for roughly 2% of all human carbon-dioxide emissions, so even small gains are enormous.
Quantum computers can simulate the exact moment a nitrogen molecule splits apart on a platinum surface, the key step in that reaction.
The aim is to redesign such processes toward nearly 100% efficiency, shrinking the carbon footprint of global chemistry.
Electricity grids pour out floods of unlabelled sensor data that must be sorted to keep power flowing and equipment healthy.
Researchers ran a quantum version of k-means clustering, loading the data through angle and amplitude encoding, on real German electricity-grid records.
That quantum clustering improved balanced accuracy for grid labelling and predictive maintenance by 67.8% over the standard classical method.
Separately, ultra-sensitive cold-atom gravity sensors track underground carbon-dioxide plumes at storage sites, catching leaks with almost no drift.

body.x: k-means is the workhorse of clustering: it sorts a pile of data points into a fixed number of groups by repeatedly asking one simple question — which group centre is each point closest to?
b: which group centre is each point closest to

body.x: The loop, in plain terms
The method begins by dropping a chosen number of group centres into the data, spaced apart on purpose so they do not start on top of one another.
Every data point is then handed to whichever centre sits nearest to it, which carves the data into that many groups.
b: handed to whichever centre sits nearest

body.x: Each centre then slides to the average position of all the points that just joined it, so it settles into the true middle of its group.
These two steps — reassign, then re-centre — repeat until no point changes its group and the arrangement stops moving.
What it is quietly minimising

Underneath, the method is chasing a single score: the total spread of the groups, measured as how far each point sits from its own centre, added up across every group; a tighter, more compact set of groups scores lower, and lower is better.

The costly part is the reassign step, because it must measure the distance from every point to every centre on every pass, so the work grows with the number of points multiplied by the number of groups.
b: number of points multiplied by the number of groups

body.x: This distance-counting step is the exact line a quantum method later sets out to shorten.

defs.def: A method that sorts data into a set number of groups by repeatedly moving each group's centre and reassigning every point to the nearest one.
The centre of a group — simply the average position of all the points currently assigned to it.
A smarter way to place the starting centres far apart so the method is less likely to settle on a poor answer.
The single number the method tries to make as small as possible: the combined tightness of all the groups.

body.x: The same simplicity that makes k-means so popular also pins down the exact situations where it quietly returns the wrong answer.
Why it is everywhere
It is easy to explain, quick to run, and comfortably handles millions of points, which is why it sits in almost every data-analysis toolkit.
The fine print
The number of groups must be chosen before the method runs, even when the natural number of groups in the data is genuinely unknown.
It quietly assumes every group is a round, roughly equal-sized blob that a straight boundary can cleanly separate from its neighbours.
The final answer can shift depending on where the starting centres happen to land, and a few stray outliers can drag a centre away from the real crowd.
The blind spot

Because each point is always handed to the nearest centre, every boundary k-means draws is effectively a straight line; when the true groups are curved — two interleaved crescents, or one ring inside another — that straight-line thinking cuts through them and mixes them together.
b: every boundary k-means draws is effectively a straight line

body.x: The picture on this slide shows the failure directly: the real shapes are curved, but k-means carves them into flat wedges that ignore the actual structure.
This curved-data failure is the specific weakness the later quantum method is designed to repair.

defs.def: A group whose natural outline is curved or bent — like a crescent or a ring — rather than a simple round blob.
A stray data point sitting far from everything else that can distort an average and pull a centre off course.
Two groups that can be cleanly divided by a single straight line or flat plane.

body.x: k-means is only the opening move; a family of other classical methods each fixes one of its weaknesses, and the last of them opens the door to quantum.
The richest of these, spectral clustering, is the one that carries straight into the quantum part of the session.

defs.def: A bottom-up method that starts with every point alone and keeps merging the two closest groups into a tree.
A method that finds clusters as dense crowds of points and labels anything in the sparse gaps as noise.
A method that describes data as a blend of overlapping bell-shaped clouds and lets a point partly belong to several at once.
A method that reshapes data using the maths of a similarity network so that curved groups become easy to separate.

cards: This method builds a family tree of the data by starting with every point on its own and repeatedly merging the two closest groups.
The result is a branching diagram, called a dendrogram, that shows how points join up at every level of closeness.
A real advantage is that the number of groups need not be fixed in advance; the tree can simply be cut wherever it is useful.
The price is that comparing and merging all the groups grows steeply as the dataset gets larger.
DBSCAN groups points by density, treating any place where many points crowd close together as a single cluster.
Because it follows the crowd rather than a fixed centre, it can trace clusters of any shape, including curved and irregular ones.
Points sitting alone in sparse regions are labelled as noise instead of being forced into a group, which makes it good at spotting outliers.
It does need a sense of scale — how close counts as close — to be set beforehand.
A Gaussian Mixture Model treats the data as a blend of several overlapping bell-shaped clouds.
Instead of assigning each point to exactly one group, it gives soft memberships, so a point can belong seventy per cent to one cloud and thirty per cent to another.
It learns the position and spread of each cloud through a patient back-and-forth refining process.
This softness makes it more flexible than k-means whenever real groups overlap at their edges.
Spectral clustering first builds a similarity graph, linking each point to its neighbours by how alike they are.
It then rewrites that graph as a large table of similarities and extracts its most informative directions through a linear-algebra step.
In this reshaped space the curved groups that defeated k-means become straight and easy, so a simple method can finish the job.
It is the first method here that handles curved shapes gracefully, which is exactly the case where k-means fails.
Its heavy lifting is pure linear algebra on a large similarity table — the very operation a quantum computer performs natively, making this the natural bridge to quantum.

body.x: Spectral clustering is the classical method that finally handles curved data, and the way it pulls that off is exactly what makes it the stepping stone into quantum.
The trick: change the space, not the method

The core move is to stop describing each point by its raw position and instead describe it by which other points it is close to; this turns the dataset into a network of connections, where being alike simply means being linked.

From that network the method extracts a handful of its most informative patterns — the directions along which the network naturally wants to split — and re-plots every point using those patterns as fresh coordinates.
In this redrawn space the curves flatten: two interleaved crescents that no straight line could separate become two plain, well-spaced blobs that ordinary k-means can then finish.
The picture on this slide captures it — data that is hopelessly tangled on one side becomes cleanly separable once it is lifted into the new space on the other.
Why this points at quantum
The expensive part is a single heavy piece of linear algebra carried out on a large table that records how similar every pair of points is.
A quantum computer is, at its core, a machine built for precisely this kind of linear algebra: manipulating large tables of numbers is its native operation rather than an add-on.

The whole pipeline can stay intact while a quantum-built measure of similarity is swapped in, one that can be richer than any straight-line distance and can expose structure a classical measure would miss.
Only the similarity changes; everything else in the pipeline stays the same, which is why this is the cleanest bridge into the quantum part.

defs.def: A network that links each data point to the others it most resembles, turning a plain dataset into a web of connections.
The branch of maths that manipulates large grids of numbers all at once — the core operation both spectral clustering and quantum computers rely on.
The natural, most informative directions hidden inside a grid of numbers, which spectral clustering uses as new coordinates for the data.
A quantum recipe that turns each data point into a quantum state, giving a new and potentially richer way to measure how similar two points are.

body.x: Before reaching for quantum, it pays to see exactly where classical clustering spends its money, because the bill always lands in the same place.
Climbing the cost ladder
The ladder on this slide climbs from cheap to ruinous; at the bottom, comparing two individual points is quick and barely depends on how much data there is.
One full pass of k-means is still manageable, since its cost rises in step with the number of points, the number of groups, and the number of features — a plain multiplication.

Building a full table of how similar every point is to every other point is far worse, because the number of pairs grows with the square of the number of points, so ten times the data means roughly a hundred times the work.
b: the square of the number of points

body.x: Analysing that table with the heavy linear-algebra step is worse again, growing with the cube of the number of points, which is what makes it buckle at large scale.
b: the cube of the number of points

body.x: The common thread

Every rung that hurts is doing one of just two things: measuring similarity between many high-dimensional points, or performing linear algebra on a large point-by-point table; nothing else on the ladder is expensive.
That is no accident — it is precisely the narrow corner where quantum methods claim leverage, since measuring overlaps and handling large tables of numbers is what quantum hardware does naturally.
b: the narrow corner where quantum methods claim leverage

body.x: With the costly corner pinned down, the case for a quantum approach is earned rather than merely assumed.

defs.def: A shorthand for how the work grows as a dataset gets bigger — whether it rises gently, squares, or cubes.
Doubling the data makes the work roughly four times larger, and ten times the data makes it about a hundred times larger.
Doubling the data makes the work roughly eight times larger — the kind of growth that stalls at scale.
A large grid holding how alike every point is to every other point — the object the expensive linear-algebra steps chew on.

body.x: Two walls stop classical clustering once data becomes genuinely large and complex, and together they explain why a quantum measure of similarity starts to look attractive.
Wall one — the curse of dimensionality

When each point carries only a few features, the ideas of near and far stay meaningful; but as the number of features climbs into the hundreds or thousands, something strange happens to distance itself.
In very high-dimensional data the closest point and the farthest point end up almost the same distance away, so the gap between nearest and farthest nearly disappears.

Once everything looks roughly equidistant, the whole idea of a nearest neighbour loses its meaning, and any method leaning on straight-line distance — k-means included — quietly stops telling points apart.
Wall two — the kernel-trick wall

The classical repair is the kernel trick: rather than comparing points directly, it treats them as if lifted into a much richer space where curved groups become separable, using a chosen similarity formula.
The catch is bookkeeping, because the trick needs a full point-by-point table of similarities whose memory grows with the square of the number of points, so it crashes on large datasets.
b: grows with the square of the number of points

body.x: This is the precise opening for a quantum kernel: a similarity that can be richer than the classical one and is produced by the physics of the device instead of stored in a giant classical table.
b: produced by the physics of the device

body.x: The problem now reads as a need for a better and cheaper measure of similarity — exactly what the quantum part sets out to deliver.

defs.def: As data gains more and more features, points spread so thin that near and far blur together and distance stops being useful.
Everyday straight-line distance between two points — the ruler measurement most clustering methods use to judge closeness.
A classical shortcut that compares points as if they had been lifted into a richer space, so curved groups become separable.
The full point-by-point table of similarities the kernel trick must hold in memory, which balloons with the square of the dataset size.

body.x: The field is racing on two fronts at once: building machines whose errors shrink instead of snowball, and letting artificial intelligence and quantum computing rescue each other's weak spots.
Behind every headline speed-up sits one quiet gatekeeper — how fast ordinary data can be loaded into the machine.

defs.def: The quantum version of a bit: instead of being only 0 or 1, it can hold a blend of both at once until it is measured.
A machine built so that small hardware mistakes do not wreck the overall calculation.
One reliable qubit made out of many error-prone physical qubits working together so their errors cancel instead of adding up.
A proposed quantum memory that loads many pieces of ordinary data into a quantum computer at the same time, rather than one at a time.

cards: Today's quantum chips make errors so often that a long calculation drowns in noise; "fault-tolerant" means spreading one dependable qubit across many shaky physical ones so their mistakes cancel out instead of piling up.
IBM has published a dated plan for a machine called Starling in 2029 that would hold 200 of these dependable qubits and run 100 million operations, with a larger successor, Blue Jay, aimed at 2,000.
Google is targeting its first genuinely useful error-corrected machine around 2029, and expects to need roughly a million physical qubits to reach it.
Quantinuum and the photonics firm PsiQuantum have their own dated targets for the same window, so several independent groups now point at the end of this decade rather than a vague someday.
Artificial intelligence and quantum computing have stopped being separate stories and now feed each other directly.

Google DeepMind's AlphaQubit, published in Nature in 2024, is a neural network that reads a quantum chip's noisy error signals and works out which faults actually happened, decoding them 6 to 30 percent more accurately than the previous best method.
That same kind of AI helper is being used to squeeze useful results out of today's noisy machines while true error correction is still years away.
Running the other way, the quantum feature maps seen earlier in this session hand ordinary machine-learning methods a new kind of similarity to work with.
The catch behind almost every promised quantum speed-up is mundane: getting the everyday data into the machine fast enough to matter.
The proposed fix is QRAM, a special quantum memory that can hand many data points to the processor at once; without it, the largest speed-ups stay on paper.
In June 2026 a group at Zhejiang University built the first working superconducting version of one QRAM design, addressing 4-bit and 8-bit data as an early proof of concept.
Progress on this single problem is what would turn the exponential speed-ups from a promise into a working tool.

body.x: Every roadmap in this session runs into the same wall, and it is not a machine — it is people who understand how the whole stack fits together.
Hardware is being built at speed across the world, but the far scarcer resource is trained minds who can turn that hardware into working software and applications.
The shortage, in numbers
Industry projects roughly 250,000 new quantum roles by 2030 and about 840,000 by 2035.
Trained supply is tiny — in 2025 there were about three job openings for every qualified hire, and roughly half of that year's roles went unfilled.
b: three job openings for every qualified hire
half of that year's roles went unfilled

body.x: Why a faculty room is the fix
This is a teaching-and-training gap, not a hardware gap, which makes it the one bottleneck a classroom can close directly.
Each new course seeded and each strong student pointed toward quantum machine learning turns into India's comparative advantage compounding year on year.
The value-capturing layer is algorithms and software, and that layer is built by people a university trains — not bought off a shelf.

A small quantum circuit that measures how alike two data points are, returning a number near 1 when they are almost identical.
A mathematical recipe that scores how similar two data points are, which clustering methods use to decide what belongs together.

cards: Clustering, kernels and the swap test can all be taught from first principles with no quantum hardware at all — the ideas in this session and a laptop are enough.
A single quantum-machine-learning module can be added to an existing machine-learning or data-science course without rebuilding the whole syllabus.

Strong students can be pointed toward the National Quantum Mission hubs and toward internships at firms such as QpiAI and BosonQ Psi.
