body.x: The swap test is a tiny quantum circuit whose only job is to measure how alike two pieces of data are, and that single skill is the beating heart of quantum clustering.
b: measure how alike two pieces of data are

body.x: Why similarity is the whole game
Clustering is really just sorting things by how alike they are, so every method keeps asking the same question over and over: how similar are these two points?
The swap test answers exactly that question using one spare qubit, called an ancilla, that acts like a referee comparing two data points and reporting back how close they are.
The intuition: interference
The trick relies on interference, the same wave behaviour that makes ripples on a pond reinforce where they meet or flatten out where they clash.
The referee qubit is set up so the two data points are quietly compared against each other, and whether that comparison reinforces or cancels shifts the odds of what the referee will read out.

When the two points are nearly identical the waves reinforce strongly, and when the points are completely unrelated the waves partly cancel, so the outcome the referee reports is a direct read-out of their similarity.

A single reading gives only one yes-or-no answer, so the circuit is run many times and the fraction of one particular answer is counted, and that fraction sits near one for alike points and near one-half for unrelated ones.
The formula printed on the diagram is only this paragraph rewritten in symbols; the plain words carry the entire idea.

defs.def: A small quantum circuit whose whole job is to report how similar two pieces of data are, boiled down to one measurable number.
A spare helper qubit added to a circuit not to store data but to run or read off a calculation, here acting as a referee that reports similarity.
The quantum version of a bit: instead of being only 0 or 1, it can hold a blend of both at once until it is measured.
The wave effect where two possibilities reinforce each other or cancel out, the same way ripples on a pond can add up or flatten.

body.x: The same swap test, walked one step at a time, is exactly what runs on the quantum computer to turn two data points into a single similarity number.
The recipe, step by step
First, the two data points are loaded into two separate groups of qubits, and one extra referee qubit is set to its starting zero position.
A gate called a Hadamard puts the referee into an even blend of its two settings at once, so it is poised to compare the points rather than being committed to any one answer.
A controlled-swap gate then tries to swap the two data points depending on the referee, and because the referee is in a blend, both swapped and not-swapped happen together and are made to interfere.
A second Hadamard folds those two possibilities back together, and only then is the referee qubit finally measured.
Reading the answer
The chance the referee lands back on zero is the number that matters, and it is highest when the two points are alike and lowest when they are unrelated.

If the two points are identical the referee returns zero almost every time, and if they are completely unrelated it returns zero only about half the time, with the whole scale in between reading off the similarity.

The quiet marvel is that this circuit stays the same tiny size no matter how many features each data point carries, which is why Lloyd, Mohseni and Rebentrost used it in 2013 as the seed of quantum k-means.
Running the circuit once is never enough; it is repeated many times and the answers are tallied, because a single quantum measurement reveals only a glimpse of the underlying similarity.

defs.def: A basic quantum operation that puts a qubit into an even fifty-fifty blend of its two settings at once.
A gate that swaps two things only when a controlling qubit says so; because that control can be in a blend, both swapped and not-swapped happen together.
Two data points that are completely unrelated and share nothing in common, the exact opposite of identical.
A quantum version of the familiar k-means clustering method that measures distances with the swap test instead of ordinary arithmetic.

body.x: Quantum k-means keeps the familiar k-means recipe exactly as it is and swaps out only one part, the step where distances are measured, replacing arithmetic with the swap test.
What actually changes

The looping logic is untouched: pick starting centres, assign every point to its nearest centre, move each centre to the average of its members, and repeat, the very same dance classical k-means already does.
The one substitution is in the assign step, where the classical straight-line distance is replaced by a swap-test similarity computed on data that has been loaded into quantum states.

Because the data now lives in a quantum state, the comparison happens inside an enormously larger space than the original spreadsheet columns, which is exactly what the feature-space row of the table is pointing at.
Why the scaling row matters
Classical k-means must, in effect, walk past every point one by one, so its cost grows in a straight line with the number of points: double the data and the work doubles.

With an ideal quantum memory feeding the circuit, the cost of a single distance can in principle shrink to something that grows only with the logarithm of the data size, so the sheer count of points barely moves the needle.
This q-means framework was set out by Kerenidis, Landman, Luongo and Prakash, and later sharpened to give a solid quadratic runtime improvement over the best comparable classical method.
Every one of those speed claims carries an asterisk: it assumes a fast quantum memory, QRAM, that does not yet exist at scale, which is why the honest reality-check follows next.

defs.def: A quantum redo of ordinary k-means clustering that keeps the same loop but measures similarity with a quantum circuit.
The average position of all the points in a cluster, its centre of gravity, which each point is compared against.
A hoped-for fast quantum memory that could load ordinary data into a quantum state quickly; it does not yet exist at useful scale.
The very large mathematical space a quantum state lives in, far bigger than the handful of columns in the original data.

body.x: The second, more practical route does not try to make k-means faster at all; it changes what similar even means, building a richer notion of similarity that a classical computer struggles to copy.
The idea in one move
Instead of speeding up the old distance, a quantum circuit is used to invent a new one, deliberately chosen so that patterns invisible to ordinary distance become obvious.
Each data point is pushed through a quantum feature map, a short circuit that spreads the point out into a vast quantum space where tangled shapes can come apart cleanly.
The swap test then measures how alike every pair of points is in that new space, and all those similarity numbers are collected into a single grid called the kernel matrix.
Why this is the near-term winner
That grid is exactly the ingredient the clustering methods faculty already teach, such as spectral clustering, expect to be handed, so the quantum part slots straight into a familiar pipeline.
The circuits involved are shallow and forgiving of today's noisy hardware, which means this approach actually runs on real machines now, not in some hoped-for future.
On the heatmap the payoff is visible as clean square blocks along the diagonal: points that belong together score high with each other, and a good kernel makes those blocks stand out sharply.
The bet behind the whole approach is that the quantum feature map reaches a space genuinely expensive to fake on a classical computer; where it does not, an ordinary kernel would do just as well.

defs.def: A short quantum circuit that spreads each data point out into a huge quantum space where tangled patterns can come apart cleanly.
A grid holding the similarity score for every pair of data points, the single ingredient most clustering methods actually need.
A classical method that groups data by studying the structure of its similarity graph, able to separate curved, interlocking shapes that k-means cannot.
Short for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum: today's real quantum computers, still small and error-prone, so circuits have to stay short.

body.x: A short live notebook makes the whole argument concrete: on a dataset shaped like two interlocking crescents, plain k-means fails and the quantum kernel succeeds.
b: plain k-means fails and the quantum kernel succeeds

body.x: The setup
The notebook, called qml_clustering_demo, builds two interleaved half-moons of points, a classic shape that curves back on itself so that no straight line can separate the two groups.
A small two-qubit circuit, feeding each point in more than once for extra expressiveness, turns every pair of points into a quantum-kernel similarity score.
Only each point's closest neighbours are kept, forming a sparse similarity graph, which is then handed to standard spectral clustering.
The verdict, in one number
The quality of a grouping is scored by a standard measure that reaches one for a perfect match to the true crescents and sits near zero for random guessing.
Ordinary k-means scores only about 0.48, because its straight-line thinking slices clean through both crescents and cuts each moon in half.
The quantum-kernel spectral method scores about 0.67, recovering the two crescents that k-means could not see, and it does so on two qubits, on an ordinary laptop, with no quantum hardware at all.
This is a curated example whose geometry happens to favour the quantum kernel; honest teaching always pits it against a strong classical baseline before claiming any advantage.

defs.def: A score for how well a grouping matches the true answer: one means a perfect match, and near zero means no better than random guessing.
A similarity score between two data points computed by a quantum circuit, designed to reveal patterns ordinary distance misses.
A trick of feeding each data point into the circuit more than once, which lets a small circuit express richer patterns.
A classical grouping method that reads the structure of a similarity graph and can separate curved, interlocking shapes.

body.x: Today's material is not borrowed theory: the presenter, Archit Srivastava, has already built and run these circuits, and his 2021 paper Quantum Finance, An Overview shows the same machinery at work.
b: already built and run these circuits

body.x: What the paper did
Srivastava's team took a classic finance problem, choosing which handful of assets to hold to balance return against risk, and rewrote it in a form a quantum computer can chew on.
The problem was folded into an energy landscape, with the rule about how many assets to pick added as a penalty, so that the best portfolio corresponds to the lowest point of that landscape.
A variational quantum circuit, tuned step by step to slide downhill toward that lowest point, was then used to find the winning selection.
Two frameworks, one physics
The very same problem was coded twice, once in IBM's Qiskit and once in Xanadu's PennyLane, which is precisely the framework choice this room of teachers now faces.
The Qiskit run landed on its best portfolio with about ninety-four percent confidence, and the PennyLane run converged to its own optimal selection, with both reaching the same underlying answer.
Why it connects to today

The tunable circuit used there is the same species of circuit as the quantum feature map used for clustering: one is adjusted to find a lowest energy, the other is read out to measure similarity, but the hardware building blocks are identical.
The lesson for this room is continuity: the circuit optimised to solve a portfolio is a close cousin of the circuit evaluated to cluster data, so one skill set carries across both.

defs.def: A method that tunes a quantum circuit step by step to find the lowest-energy answer to a problem.
A way of rewriting a decision problem as an energy landscape, so the best answer becomes the lowest point to roll down to.
IBM's free software toolkit for writing and running quantum programs.
Xanadu's free software toolkit for quantum computing and quantum machine learning, used in today's clustering notebook.

body.x: Honesty is what earns a technical room's trust, so the speedups just described come with two large asterisks that must be stated plainly.
The data-loading bottleneck
A quantum computer can only work its magic once the classical data is inside it, and getting the data in is far harder than it sounds.
The dramatic, exponential speedup for quantum k-means quietly assumes a special fast quantum memory that can load data into a quantum state almost instantly.
That memory, known as QRAM, does not yet exist at any useful scale, so the assumption has to be spoken aloud every single time a speedup is claimed, not buried in a footnote.
b: does not yet exist at any useful scale

body.x: The dequantization warning
Even where the hardware existed, some quantum advantages turned out to be less special than they first appeared.

Starting in 2018, the computer scientist Ewin Tang and others showed that several exponentially faster quantum results could be matched by ordinary classical algorithms, once those classical methods were allowed the same shortcuts for sampling data.
The durable lesson is that a genuine quantum advantage lives only where the quantum similarity itself is truly hard to fake classically, not merely where the data-loading was assumed away.
Stating these limits openly does not weaken the case; a room of researchers respects the candour far more than an oversold promise.

defs.def: A hoped-for fast quantum memory that would load ordinary data into a quantum state; the big speedups assume it, but it does not yet exist at scale.
Showing that a supposedly quantum-only speedup can actually be matched by an ordinary classical computer once the classical method is given the same shortcuts.
A dramatic kind of speed gain where the quantum method's effort barely grows even as the problem gets vastly bigger.

body.x: With the caveats on the table, here is where the quantum advantage is real today, where it is not, and how to say it in one honest sentence.
b: where the quantum advantage is real today

defs.def: Short for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum: today's real quantum computers, small and error-prone, so useful circuits have to stay short.
A training trap where a deep, over-flexible quantum circuit gives almost no signal about which way to adjust it, so the learning stalls.
A similarity score between data points computed by a quantum circuit, richer than ordinary straight-line distance.
Using the quantum circuit only to invent a better similarity, then handing that off to the classical clustering methods people already use.

cards: Today's quantum machines are noisy and can only run short circuits before errors pile up, which is exactly why the clustering methods worth teaching now keep their circuits shallow.
Shallow quantum kernels are attractive precisely because they tolerate a little noise instead of demanding perfect hardware.
A second trap called a barren plateau strikes deep, over-flexible circuits: their tuning signal flattens out almost everywhere, so the training gets lost with no downhill direction to follow.
The practical rule is to keep circuits shallow and structured, which sidesteps both the noise and the plateau at once.
The honest sweet spot is data that is already quantum in nature or has rich structure, where a quantum description fits naturally rather than being forced onto it.
It also shows up in small but genuinely hard similarity problems, where a classical kernel struggles yet the dataset is modest enough to run today.
The most reliable role right now is as a feature generator: the quantum circuit invents a richer similarity, and the familiar classical clustering methods do the rest.
CERN's unsupervised clustering of collision data is the clearest case so far of a quantum model genuinely beating classical on real hardware.
Quantum clustering is not k-means running faster on the spreadsheet sitting on a laptop today.
It is a genuinely new kind of similarity, one that can run on real hardware right now and feed straight into the methods faculty already teach.
The dramatic asymptotic speedups are real on paper but still wait on better hardware and on a working quantum memory.
Said plainly: not faster arithmetic, but a different and richer way to measure how alike two things are.

body.x: The largest scientific instruments humanity has ever built — radio-telescope arrays, particle colliders, neutrino detectors and gravitational-wave observatories — all share one problem: they produce a flood of measurements with no answer key attached.

A single radio array like the SKA pours out data faster than the entire global internet and archives it by the exabyte, the collider at CERN stores collision data in the same exabyte range, the DUNE neutrino experiment records tens of petabytes of 3D images every year, and LIGO watches a four-kilometre ruler shift by less than one ten-thousandth of the width of a proton.
b: faster than the entire global internet

body.x: Because almost none of that data arrives pre-sorted into known categories, making sense of it is fundamentally a task of finding structure without labels — exactly what clustering and anomaly detection are built to do.

defs.def: Measurements that arrive with no answer key, so nobody has marked which ones are signal, which are noise, and which might be a discovery.
A unit of storage equal to a billion billion bytes, roughly a million times the capacity of a large laptop.
Automatically grouping data points that resemble one another, without being told in advance what the groups are.
Automatically spotting the rare data points that do not fit any of the usual patterns.

body.x: LIGO listens for gravitational waves — ripples in space itself — by measuring how a four-kilometre laser beam stretches by an almost unimaginably tiny amount. What ultimately limits it is not better engineering but the quantum fuzziness of light.
The quantum limit

Even a perfect laser is grainy: its light arrives in a restless jitter set by the quantum vacuum, and that jitter blurs the measurement. This floor is called the standard quantum limit, and for decades it capped how faint a signal the detector could hear.

Squeezed light is engineered light in which that unavoidable quantum jitter is pushed out of the property being measured and into a property that does not matter, leaving the measurement quieter than the vacuum normally allows.
The A+ upgrade

The A+ upgrade injects a specially prepared quantum vacuum through a new 300-metre filter cavity, so the squeezing can be tuned separately at every frequency the detector cares about — low notes and high notes both get quieter.
The measured quantum-noise reduction reached 4.0 decibels at the Hanford site and 5.8 decibels at the Livingston site, an improvement that holds from tens of hertz up into the kilohertz range.

That extra sensitivity stretches each detector's reach by about 15 to 18 percent, which raises the rate of detected black-hole and neutron-star mergers by as much as 65 percent during the fourth observing run.
This is quantum technology used not to compute but to measure, and every extra detection it buys becomes fresh, unlabelled data for the clustering pipelines downstream.

defs.def: Tiny ripples in the fabric of space and time, set off by violent events such as two black holes colliding.
The natural noise floor set by the graininess of light itself, below which an ordinary measurement cannot reach.
Specially prepared light whose unavoidable quantum jitter is shifted away from the quantity being measured, making that measurement sharper.
A ratio scale for comparing signal strengths; here, more decibels means more of the quantum noise has been removed.

body.x: A gravitational-wave detector is so sensitive that it also picks up thousands of unrelated noise blips, called glitches, that can hide or imitate a real signal. Sorting those blips is itself an unsupervised-learning problem.
Cleaning the noise — Gravity Spy
Gravity Spy is a project that combines tens of thousands of human volunteers with a neural network to sort detector glitches by their shape, so the look-alikes can be grouped together and removed.
The effort has organised glitches into 23 visual classes, drawing on more than 30,000 volunteers and over 7 million individual classifications.

A newer network that fuses several views of each glitch reaches 94.1 percent accuracy, with a reliability score (the area under the ROC curve) of 0.965 — grouping noise by resemblance, which is clustering in spirit.
The quantum angle and the Indian detector

A quantum machine-learning method called quantum variational rewinding treats each reading as a possible anomaly against a learned model of the background hum, and does so in time that grows only linearly with the data — faster in principle than the quasilinear matched-filtering used today.
LIGO-India broke ground on 23 April 2026 at Aundha in Maharashtra and will add a third pair of four-kilometre arms, aiming to switch on around 2030 at a cost of roughly 2,600 crore rupees.
A third detector on a different continent sharpens the whole network's ability to pinpoint where in the sky each signal came from.
None of these steps needs a pre-labelled catalogue of glitches or signals; the structure has to be discovered from the data itself.

defs.def: A brief burst of instrument noise that can mimic or bury a genuine gravitational-wave signal.
A computer model loosely inspired by the brain that learns to recognise patterns from examples.
The standard way of finding a signal by sliding a library of expected waveforms across the data to look for a match.
A single score from 0 to 1 for how well a classifier separates two categories; 1.0 is perfect and 0.5 is a coin flip.

body.x: At CERN's Large Hadron Collider, protons are smashed together hundreds of millions of times a second, and almost every collision is already-understood physics. Any genuinely new physics would show up as a rare event that does not match the familiar patterns — an anomaly.
The headline unsupervised win
CERN set up its Quantum Technology Initiative in 2020, and the standout result so far sits in exactly this kind of pattern-free search.

A neural network called an autoencoder first squeezes each messy collision into a short numerical fingerprint, and a quantum kernel then measures how alike those fingerprints are so a quantum version of k-means and k-medians can group them.

On real quantum hardware this quantum clustering was found to significantly outperform the classical version in a specific regime — the clearest genuine advantage for unsupervised quantum clustering shown on hardware to date (Communications Physics, 2024).
The rest of the toolbox
Working out which particle made which track was rewritten as a QUBO — an optimisation puzzle a quantum annealer can attack — and run on a D-Wave machine with 33 qubits over about 500 tracks.
An annealing-inspired classical method then sped that same task up by roughly four orders of magnitude, showing that quantum ideas can pay off even on ordinary computers.
A quantum support-vector machine hunting the Higgs signature matched classical accuracy at up to 20 qubits and 50,000 events — solid parity rather than a breakthrough.
The upgraded collider will store data in the exabyte range and demand 50 to 100 times today's computing, pressure that keeps unsupervised methods central.

defs.def: A neural network that compresses each data point into a short fingerprint capturing its essential features, and the compact space those fingerprints live in.
A way of measuring how similar two data points are by encoding them into a quantum state so the physics itself performs the comparison.
A puzzle written as choosing yes or no for many switches to make a score as low as possible, a form a quantum annealer can solve.
A special-purpose quantum machine that finds low-energy solutions by slowly settling into them, well suited to optimisation puzzles.

body.x: Fusion reactors try to bottle a gas hotter than the core of the Sun inside powerful magnetic fields. That gas, a plasma, is twitchy and can collapse in an instant, so keeping it stable is a control and pattern-recognition problem.
AI keeping the plasma alive

ITER is the giant international reactor being built to prove that fusion can produce far more energy than it consumes, and machine learning is already steering the smaller machines that lead up to it.

In a 2022 result, DeepMind and EPFL used reinforcement learning to command a tokamak's magnetic coils directly and hold several different plasma shapes — the first time such learning controlled the magnets of a real fusion machine.
On the DIII-D tokamak in 2024, a reinforcement-learning controller forecast a dangerous tearing instability up to 300 milliseconds ahead and adjusted the machine to stay stable.
A predictor trained on two tokamaks in 2019 could warn of collapses on a third machine it had never seen — vital because ITER may get only about 30 milliseconds of warning.
Sorting states, and the quantum role

Unsupervised classification sorts the plasma into its natural operating regimes — the calm low-confinement and high-confinement modes and the dangerous disruptive ones — without being told the categories in advance.

On the quantum side, algorithms are being written for plasma turbulence and transport, while diamond-based NV-centre magnetometers promise radiation-hard sensors that keep working close to the reactor.
ITER's revised plan targets producing at least ten times the energy it consumes by 2044.

defs.def: A superheated, electrically charged gas — the fourth state of matter that fuels stars and fusion reactors.
A doughnut-shaped chamber that uses magnetic fields to hold a fusion plasma away from the walls.
A kind of machine learning where a controller learns by trial and error, rewarded whenever its actions lead to a good outcome.
A sudden loss of control in which the fusion plasma collapses, potentially damaging the machine.

body.x: Two more mega-experiments generate data far too vast to sort by hand, so both lean on pipelines that find structure without any labels at all.

defs.def: A tiny, almost massless particle that streams through ordinary matter, and even whole planets, almost without stopping.
Unwanted human-made radio signals — phones, satellites, engines — that contaminate a radio telescope's data.
A neural network that learns to compress data into a compact fingerprint and rebuild it, useful for spotting whatever does not fit.
A clustering method that groups points packed closely together and labels isolated points as noise, without being told how many groups to expect.

cards: The SKA is a radio-telescope array so large its antennas number over a hundred thousand on the low-frequency side alone, all working together as one instrument.
It streams data at around 8 terabits per second and archives more than 700 petabytes a year, building toward roughly 8.5 exabytes — over 100 times the world's current internet traffic.
Because nobody can hand-label that flood, an autoencoder paired with a nearest-neighbour search flags radio interference automatically, and the DBSCAN algorithm groups the false alarms together.
A quantum-kernel classifier sorting galaxies into spiral versus elliptical scored 0.946 on its accuracy curve, statistically tied with the best classical method.
Its first image in March 2025 captured 85 galaxies using under 1 percent of the planned antennas.
DUNE studies neutrinos, near-ghostly particles, using four giant tanks of liquid argon totalling 70,000 tonnes, buried about 1.5 kilometres underground.
It will record 30 to 60 petabytes a year, with every neutrino event captured as a detailed 3D image of particle tracks.
Clustering is used to pull apart overlapping tracks and particle showers, while neural networks reconstruct what actually happened.

A quantum method combining projected quantum kernels with a quantum convolutional network told straight tracks from spray-like cascades about 80 percent of the time on IBM's 127-qubit hardware, on par with classical tools.
Assembly is underway, with the huge cryostat's steel lowered underground in 2026.

body.x: Quantum sensing turns delicate quantum states of atoms and light into measuring instruments of extraordinary precision. Each one is also a new source of rich, unlabelled data.
Feeling gravity and time
By dropping ultra-cold atoms and reading their quantum wave nature, these devices measure forces and time far more finely than any classical instrument can.

A cold-atom gravity sensor built in Birmingham made the first outdoor detection of a buried tunnel about a metre underground, promising surveys roughly ten times faster and a way to watch carbon-dioxide plumes at underground storage sites.
b: first outdoor detection of a buried tunnel

body.x: The most advanced optical atomic clocks are so steady they would drift by less than a second over the entire age of the universe, precise enough to sense a few centimetres of height difference through Einstein's relativity.
b: less than a second over the entire age of the universe

body.x: Magnetism and the hunt for the invisible
A class of optical magnetometers can now feel magnetic fields around ten billion times weaker than the Earth's, surpassing the superconducting SQUID sensors that long held the record.

Kilometre-scale atom interferometers such as MAGIS-100, AION and AEDGE are being built to hunt gravitational waves and dark matter — the unseen material thought to make up most of the universe's mass — in a frequency band that sits between what space-based and ground-based observatories can hear.
Every one of these instruments streams out ultra-precise measurements that, like the mega-science data before them, arrive without labels and become clustering's next raw material.

defs.def: Using the fragile quantum behaviour of atoms or light as an extraordinarily sensitive measuring device.
The most accurate clock ever made, keeping time by the ultra-fast vibration of atoms illuminated with laser light.
An instrument that splits and recombines the wave nature of atoms to measure tiny forces, motions, or ripples in space.
A superconducting sensor that was, until recently, the gold standard for detecting extremely weak magnetic fields.

body.x: Every one of these giant experiments tells the same story: they emit enormous, extreme-dimensional data that nobody has labelled, which is precisely the home turf of clustering and anomaly detection.
b: extreme-dimensional data that nobody has labelled

body.x: Quantum technology helps from both ends — quantum kernels and annealers embed the data in vast mathematical spaces to expose hidden structure, while quantum sensors create an entirely new class of ultra-precise measurements that themselves become fresh unlabelled data streams.

The honest caveat: across astronomy, neutrinos and gravitational waves most quantum-machine-learning results so far merely match the best classical methods — the one clear win on real hardware is CERN's unsupervised clustering of collision events.

defs.def: A way to gauge the similarity between two data points by encoding them into quantum states so the physics performs the comparison.
A special-purpose quantum machine that solves optimisation puzzles by slowly settling into a low-energy answer.
Performing equally well, no better and no worse, than the existing classical method.
